Kit calls it "a near-perfect blend of violence, vulnerability, and vision." It’s one of her personal top five films of all time. Cade, however, wasn’t convinced. "I’m not a Tarantino fan," he confessed. "It’s too self-indulgent for me." Still, even he acknowledged the film’s cultural weight, from the yellow tracksuit to the unforgettable showdown with the Crazy 88. "It’s iconic," he said, "just not for me."

Kill Bill: Vol. 1 is a rollicking, action-packed tribute to martial arts cinema, overflowing with gore, inventive fight choreography, and a funky, unforgettable soundtrack. Tarantino revels in stylized combat, creating sequences that are both visually stunning and wildly entertaining. However, the film is not without flaws: some performances are underwhelming, and the dialogue, while occasionally sharp, lacks the punch and wit audiences have come to expect from Tarantino. Still, as a celebration of genre cinema and kinetic spectacle, Kill Bill: Vol. 1 is fun, exhilarating, and impossible to ignore — a thrilling ride for fans of action and stylistic excess.

An absolutely stellar film, with in your face action sequences. It can get a bit cheesy at times, but that's just part of the charm. I thought Lucy Liu and Uma Thurman both did fantastic, and RZA's soundtrack just brought it over the top. 8/10
Loved the sequences and how the film looks from different angles. Great picture quality for 2003
I can’t believe I slept on this movie for so many years, this is honestly a new favorite of mine, and I would gladly check out more of Quentin Tarantino‘s work.

Kill Bill: Vol. 1 is a ridiculously fun and wild movie. The characters are well-written. I love the way they are introduced. Hattori Hanzo has to be the coolest name and character in any Tarantino movie. The Bride is a good character too, but I just didn't connect with her as much. The dialogues are obviously great, like one would expect. But I didn't enjoy it as much as in his other movies. Overall I think the writing is great, but not as good as I hoped it to based on other Tarantino movies. The direction is awesome. This movie deals with heavy themes, but it has a goofy, ridiculous tone and it doesn't take itself seriously which works perfectly. The cinematography is gorgeous. The framing, lighting and camera movement create an amazing atmosphere and Tarantino uses colours as he didn't do before and I love that. The editing is awesome. The structure is complicated but not too hard to follow and this probably Tarantino's Oppenheimer in that sense. The film doesn't really need you to know when a certain scene happens chronologically, you just have to loosen up feel the vibes. Speaking of vibes, the sound design and soundtrack are wild and heavily stylized. The stunt work and choreography are insane, while the visual effects are ridiculously stylized. The cast is great, there's fame, talent and fit. The acting performances are great, Lucy Liu and Uma Thurman stand out for me. Kill Bill: Vol. 1 is a wild ride and one of a kind (until the second movie comes out).

This was always my favorite of the two volumes in the Kill Bill franchise. They stylish epic action sequences in this film, especially in the dojo, is beyond incredible. The story laid out here is a beautiful story of revenge, and the cinematic art direction that Quentin Tarantino takes is fresh and stylish.

The violent set-pieces and bloodshed take more of a "Mr. Orange" backseat - but equally bloody - ride in The Bride's second outing as Tarantino doubles down on what he does best: emphasising everything that makes his larger than life characters tick.

Quentin Tarantino’s Kill Bill: Vol. 1 is basically his teenage boy’s wet dream come to life, which comes as no surprise considering Tarantino’s love for 70’s martial arts movies. Plot and characters mean little or nothing here. The plot? A bride (Uma Thurman) seeks revenge for the killing of her wedding party. The characters? Does it really matter? What matters is the style and it is uber stylish. The fight scenes effectively mimic the chopsocky (is that appropriate nowadays?) films of yesteryear – quick cuts, gravity-defying physicality, quick zooms, exaggerated sound effects, plenty of blood, etc. The rest of the movie is the exact opposite – beautifully composed shots, effective camera movement, vibrant colours, and relatively restrained performances. It may not be up there with the best that Tarantino has to offer, but it's still pretty entertaining.
Quentin just can’t stop making amazing movies.

Kill Bill: Volume One is a film that leaves an indelible mark on anyone who watches it, though for me, the experience is bittersweet. My personal history with the film is complicated—someone once dressed as The Bride (Uma Thurman’s character) and bullied me as a child—but even with that association, I can objectively recognize the film’s artistry, cultural impact, and technical mastery. Uma Thurman delivers a career-defining performance, fully embodying The Bride with grit, intensity, and emotional depth. Her portrayal goes beyond the archetypal revenge-driven character; she’s vulnerable, determined, and compelling, making the audience invested in every punch, kick, and calculated move. Tarantino’s signature style—exaggerated action, stylized cinematography, and nonlinear storytelling—is on full display here, transforming what could have been a straightforward revenge narrative into a cinematic tapestry rich with tension, artistry, and memorable imagery. The narrative itself is deceptively simple: a woman wronged embarks on a relentless quest for vengeance. Yet the beauty lies in how Tarantino structures her journey, turning each confrontation into a character study as much as an action set piece. The fights are meticulously choreographed, blending martial arts precision with heightened cinematic flair, creating sequences that feel simultaneously brutal and balletic. The violence is never gratuitous for the sake of shock; it is purposeful, reflecting The Bride’s resilience, strategy, and humanity. A particularly notable element of the film is its emphasis on complex, strong female characters. The supporting women are not mere foils; they are formidable, multi-dimensional, and leave lasting impressions. Tarantino’s ability to give each character presence and weight ensures that even secondary figures contribute meaningfully to the story’s emotional and thematic resonance. The chemistry among the cast enhances each encounter, making confrontations, dialogue exchanges, and alliances feel genuine, even in a hyper-stylized world. Visually, Kill Bill: Volume One is striking. From the animated sequences that delve into backstory to the vibrant color palettes and innovative camera work, every frame is carefully composed to draw the viewer deeper into the film’s universe. The score complements the action perfectly, amplifying tension, drama, and emotion in equal measure. Ultimately, despite the personal associations that make my viewing experience unique, Kill Bill: Volume One stands as a masterclass in storytelling, performance, and visual design. It is a film that balances raw action with character depth, and revenge with resilience, creating an unforgettable cinematic experience. For fans of stylized action, intricate choreography, and strong, layered female leads, this film is an essential watch that continues to resonate decades after its release.

Kill Bill was the first R-rated movie I saw as a kid, the first movie that felt like it had something more to tell me, more to show me. The music, the tone, the ‘70s and ‘80s influence—it all hit me at once. Up until then, movies were mostly just colorful fun. I loved cartoons, but this felt different. It felt bigger. I needed more of that. I immediately searched the web (I was 8 so I asked my dad to) and found out who made the movie. That’s when I discovered Quentin Tarantino, and I was hooked. The over-the-top violence, the blood and gore, the cheesy one-liners that somehow still sound effortlessly cool—even today—they all worked. The movie's blend of comedy and tragedy, filtered through Tarantino’s obsession with old television and genre filmmaking, just clicked for me. The world of Kill Bill is both completely believable and completely absurd, and that balance is part of what makes it great. Uma Thurman shines as The Bride out for revenge on Bill, playing this incredibly sad and over the top character with an earnestness that someone else might have underplayed. Every fight scene is a blood-soaked, hyper-choreographed burst of joy. Every rewatch lifts my spirits. With a killer script, unforgettable characters, amazing action, and a distinct visual style, Kill Bill knows exactly what it is—and delivers on that vision with style and confidence. Only a few films have ever felt perfect to me, this one made that list with ease.

Uma Thurman portrays The Bride, also known as Beatrix Kiddo, and she is truly unstoppable. After waking up from a coma, she quickly decides to take revenge, slicing and dicing anyone foolish enough to cross her path. Lucy Liu plays O-Ren Ishii, who is icy and strategic, complete with an incredible anime backstory that’s a visual masterpiece. If revenge is best served cold, then Kill Bill delivers it with plenty of blood, sharp samurai swords, and a hefty dose of attitude. Well done, Tarantino. Well done.
